Displaced oblique fracture of shaft of right ulna, subsequent encounter for closed fracture with routine healing ( S52.231D)
PT: Eval & Treat w/ Modalities Frequency/Duration: HOME HEALTH EVAL AND TREAT: SKILLED NURSING: HOME PT RT UPPER
EXTREMITY ROM, Strengthening, 2XWK FOR 6-8WKS ADD STRETCHING AND GAIT TRAINING
on behalf of JASON A. ZARN, PA-C
The patient is a 57 year old female who presents for a follow-up visit.
Alcohol Assessment: No Current Alcohol Problem 02/01/2024

Displaced oblique fracture of shaft of right ulna, subsequent encounter for closed fracture with routine healing
History of present illness: Very pleasant 57-year-old female with history significant for severe rheumatoid arthritis, osteoporosis and
end-stage right shoulder arthritis presents to my office for reevaluation of her right shoulder and right ulnar shaft fracture. Patient
notes the date of injury for the shoulder fracture was 6 weeks ago. Date of injury for the ulnar shaft fracture was approximately 5 to
6 months ago. Patient reports the shoulder feels much better as well as the ulnar shaft fractures.
General appearance: The patient is awake, alert and oriented x4. The patient is in no apparent distress.
Physical examination: On examination of this patient's right shoulder finds no obvious gross deformity. There is expected resolved
postinjury swelling and ecchymosis to both the shoulder and the forearm. 2+ RP. No wrist drop. No sensory deficits. Brisk cap
refill. Stiff range of motion of the elbow and shoulder albeit improved from prior visit. Good motion of the forearm without pain..
Good motion of the wrist and phalanges. The patient is grossly neurovascularly intact. No pain to palpation to the forearm or the
Radiographic examination: X-rays of this patient's right shoulder 2 views finds a mildly impacted and comminuted proximal humerus
fracture with good congruency of the GH joint. End-stage arthritis is present of the GH joint. Interval bony healing seen. 2 views of
the right forearm demonstrates healed ulnar shaft fracture with serial imaging.
Very pleasant 57-year-old female has acute right shoulder proximal humerus fracture with comminution and mild impaction and right
forearm ulnar shaft fracture, stable and improved. I recommend home health PT. Patient will follow-up in 6 weeks for recheck. DC
sling. The ulna shaft fracture appears to be healed with repeat imaging. She may resume normal daily activities.
Patient does voice understanding and agrees with the above plan of care. All questions were answered to the patient's satisfaction.
The patient will follow up in clinic with myself in 6 weeks for recheck
The patient knows to call and/or return to clinic with any questions and/or concerns
The patient was seen today and examined by PA Zarn and discussed fully with Dr. Holman Chan, along with plan collaboration
Order 2 views right shoulder next visit
Thank you for allowing me to participate in the care of Mrs. Bell

Shoulder X-Ray, 2 Views (Taken and Read) - RT
Forearm X-Ray, 2 Views (Taken and Read) - RT
Electronically signed by: JASON A. ZARN PA-C
6. Left ring finger EDC tendon attritional tear.
7. Left small finger EDC tendon attritional tear.
5. Left ring finger EDC tendon repair.
6. Left small finger EDC tendon repair.
Left volar wrist mass, left index, middle, ring, and
small finger inflamed tenosynovitis with rice type
bodies encompassing all tendons; inflamed dorsal
wrist capsule globally, and attritional rupture of
the ring and small extensor tendon.
DESCRIPTION OF PROCEDURE: The patient was identified in the preoperative holding area where
the correct patient, laterality, and procedure were confirmed. The risks and benefits of surgery were
discussed and I marked the left wrist with my initials. The patient was brought to the operative suite
where she was administered general anesthesia. A non-sterile tourniquet was placed on the left upper
extremity. The left upper extremity was prepped and draped in a standard sterile fashion. A brief
time-out was performed to verify the correct patient, laterality, and procedure. All were in agreement.
An Esmarch was used and the tourniquet was inflated to 230 mmHg. I started with the volar mass. I
made a longitudinal incision along the FCR and then made another incision along the plane of the thenar
muscle centered over the mass. I incised the dermis. I spread and identified several small arterial
branches which were cauterized and mobilized. I identified the mass. I traced it down to the radiocarpal
joint capsule. I cauterized it at the stalk and sent it as a specimen to Pathology. I then explored and found
no residual mass. I irrigated the area with curette and used a 3-0 Vicryl suture to sew up the hole. This
completed my left volar mass excision.
Then, I turned my attention to the dorsal wrist/hand. There was a large swelling and protuberance over
the dorsal hand. I made a large longitudinal incision over the hand. I incised the dermis. I spread down.
There was large protuberant mass which was encompassing all the extensor tendons of the fourth
compartment. I isolated the tendons initially to the index, middle, ring, and small. The ring and small finger
tendons were completely ruptured in an attritional manner with small fibrous bands bridging the tendon.
The mass was very inflamed, protuberant tenosynovitis with gross rice bodies. As much of this was
debulked and removed as possible, I completed the tenosynovectomy of the index, middle, ring, and small
fingers. This was sent as a composite specimen to Pathology. The index and middle finger tendons were
frayed but were intact. The ring and small fingers were not. I resected the bridging synovial bands. I
trimmed and freshened up the edges of the ring and small finger tendons. I then used 4-0 MaxBraid
suture and did a four-portion repair to repair the remaining tendons back to one another. The remainder of
the bulkiness was inflamed synovium. In order to remove this, I would have to remove the capsule and I
did not feel safe or comfortable doing this. At this point, I determined I had to remove as much of the
mass in the form of tenosynovitis as I could safely do with this approach. The tourniquet was down.
Hemostasis was achieved using bipolar electrocautery. Copious amounts of sterile saline were used to
irrigate the wound. Then, 3-0 Vicryl was used for dermal layer followed by 4-0 nylon horizontal mattress
suture on both sides. Then, 0.25% Marcaine with epinephrine was administered for peri-incisional
anesthesia. A sterile dressing was applied followed by resting hand splint in a protected position. The
patient tolerated the procedure well and sustained no intraoperative complications. The patient was taken
to PACU in a stable condition.
